scenario: Nimbus Cartography exports 6000 nodes per release into a Camunda 8.8 Feelers template that renders documentation URLs. Each node has an id and a parent property pointing to its tree parent, while the top-level root field holds the shared URL prefix. The template loops over nodes and must include the top-level root in every URL, not the per-node parent value.
question: How must the loop body reference the top-level root so the per-node parent property does not shadow the outer scope accessor?

options.text: The loop body references the top-level value as parent.root, treating a single parent accessor as a walk one level up from the current node in the data
The loop body references the top-level value as this.root, treating this as a synonym for the enclosing data when no explicit scope is set in the loop
The loop body references the top-level value as _parent_.root, using the documented underscore form to bypass the per-node parent property of each item
The loop body references the top-level value as parent.parent.root, chaining two parent accessors to traverse past the node's own parent property safely

a.text: Incorrect. Because the current node already has its own parent property, the bare parent accessor resolves to that property value rather than to the outer scope, breaking the URL.

b.text: Incorrect. The this accessor refers to the current loop item, not to the enclosing data, so this.root would look for a root property on the node and not find one.

c.text: Correct. When the data already uses the parent or this keyword, the documented alternative is to wrap the accessor with underscores as _parent_ or _this_ so the scope accessor wins over the property.

d.text: Incorrect. Chaining parent twice does not address the shadowing problem and is not the documented remedy; the first parent still resolves to the node property.

explanation: When the data already contains keys named parent or this, the bare accessors collide with those properties. The documented alternative form surrounds the accessor with underscores, so _parent_.root reaches the top-level field even when each node carries its own parent value.

docs.title: Forms templating - parent and this accessors
